---
title: "Effects of Urban Green Spaces on Mental Health Outcomes"
author:
  - name: Jane Smith
    affiliations:
      - Department of Public Health, University of Example
  - name: John Doe
    affiliations:
      - Department of Psychology, University of Example
date: 2025-06-15
format: pdf
abstract: |
  Urban green spaces have been suggested to improve mental health,
  but evidence remains limited. This study examined the relationship
  between proximity to green spaces and self-reported mental health
  outcomes in a sample of 500 urban residents. Results indicate a
  moderate positive association between green space access and
  mental well-being.
---

## Introduction

Mental health disorders represent a growing public health concern in urban
environments worldwide. As urbanization continues to accelerate, understanding
the environmental determinants of mental health becomes increasingly important.

Previous research has suggested that exposure to natural environments may
have beneficial effects on psychological well-being. However, the majority
of studies have relied on cross-sectional designs, limiting causal inference.

In this study, we aimed to examine the association between residential
proximity to urban green spaces and mental health outcomes in a diverse
sample of urban residents.

## Methods

### Study Design and Participants

We conducted a cross-sectional survey of 500 adults residing in a
mid-sized European city between January and June 2024. Participants
were recruited through random sampling of residential addresses.
Inclusion criteria were: age 18 years or older, residence in the
study area for at least 12 months, and ability to complete the
survey in English.

### Measures

Mental health was assessed using the General Health Questionnaire
(GHQ-12), a widely used screening instrument for psychological
distress. Green space proximity was measured as the Euclidean
distance from each participant's residence to the nearest public
green space of at least 1 hectare.

We also collected data on potential confounders including age,
gender, income, education level, and physical activity.

### Statistical Analysis

We used multiple linear regression to examine the association
between green space proximity and GHQ-12 scores, adjusting for
potential confounders. All analyses were performed using R version
4.3.1.

## Results

Of the 500 participants, 487 provided complete data and were
included in the analysis. The mean age was 42.3 years (SD = 14.1),
and 52% were female.

In the adjusted model, each 100-meter decrease in distance to
green space was associated with a 0.8-point improvement in GHQ-12
scores (β = -0.008, 95% CI: -0.014 to -0.002, p = 0.01).

The association was stronger among participants aged 60 and older
(β = -0.015, p = 0.003) compared to younger age groups.

## Discussion

Our findings suggest that proximity to urban green spaces is
associated with better mental health outcomes. These results are
consistent with previous research highlighting the psychological
benefits of nature exposure.

Several limitations should be noted. First, the cross-sectional
design precludes causal inference. Second, we relied on
self-reported mental health measures. Third, we did not account
for the quality of green spaces.

Future research should employ longitudinal designs and incorporate
objective measures of mental health to strengthen the evidence base.

## Conclusion

This study provides evidence supporting the mental health benefits
of urban green spaces. Urban planners should consider these findings
when designing cities that promote well-being.
